text: Twist in the loop like an affinity symbol tensions belts I believe

author: @Calligraphybooster
text: Instead of the usual splice (which makes a lumb roughly twice the circumference of the rope itself) you can unravel a three tiers rope 3x the length otherwise needed, take one tier, make a loop of the required length, and start winding it around itself till you get at the end after two more full loops! Voilà: An ‘endless’ rope loop, with apparently one of the tiers cut. When you use polyprop rope, you can melt the ends together. If so wished you can do this whole procedure with the remaining two tiers.

author: @bartronicsecurity
text: The rope idea is neat but still has a problem. Remember that the blades have to be able to turn into the wind direction to get the best use of the wind. That means that the whole base also has to turn around to face the wind. So how about using a set of gears at 90 degrees. The blades has a shaft that is horizontal/ parallel to the ground and the vertical shaft sending the power down to the base. In this way the blades can swing in any direction at the top and it simply keeps in contact with the 90 degree gear at the top of the vertical shaft. Rotation/ direction problem of the blades solved and also the passing of power to the base at the same time in one simple step. The generator is simply connected to the end of the vertical shaft at the base. Enjoy

author: @ffaubert1
text: Great video. This would be fantastic for a water wheel because the stream tends not to change direction. How could you do it with wind where the head has to rotate to face the wind direction?

author: @vmoutsop
text: This is awesome but I was wondering, doesn't the added weight and friction from the rope and wheel setup also contribute to the efficiency reduction? All that extra mass requires more torque which means you need higher speed winds just to get moving.

author: @richardgregory4640
text:
Surely the 85% efficiency is from the conversion of kinetic energy to electrical energy, in which case this will remain the same wherever the generator is installed. The 90% efficiency must relate to the pulley system, so you're introducing additional losses into the system. I'm not saying there aren't situations where this arrangement won't be useful, but I can't see how it can be more efficient. 
It might also be helpful to understand how the '90%' was calculated, or was it just an educated guess?

author: @johnwilson3668
text:
I am using the same idea except I am using a drive shaft . If you fit a bevel gear on the drive wheel shaft and a corresponding bevel gear on a vertical blade fan , 
the setup with the wheels can be driven directly irrespective of which way the wind is blowing .

author: @dolanbaker
text: I am looking at buildig a vertical axis wind turbine on which I intend to use a shaft to transfer the power to the ground so the generator stays at ground level.  Easer to maintain and less weight on top.

author: @scuzyprod.1611
text:
All right, but let's say you want your wind turbine to move with the wind, (that would be the most efficient, I think). A rope would get tangled in this scenario.
 I once thought about a way to transmit mechanical power from a wind turbine down to a generator, using gears and shafts. But then again, how do you transmit power and rotate with the wind? It might be possible, but very hard to do. You have a lot of tools and materials. Can you please try and find a way to make a transmision that can rotate with the mill itself, for a future project? Thanks for reading!

author: @allanwood3562
text: I tried explaining to an acquaintance the use of old ideas in new applications the other day. To may dismay the fellow couldn't give a damn so this was an encouraging presentation vindicating my point.  When you think about water pumping windmills they always used mechanical transmission of one form or another and were relatively simple, albeit a bit risky, to service.

author: @ryanlebeck259
text:
Steel cable would accomplish the same result without stretching or slack. Teflon to Teflon(PTFE) working surfaces would reduce friction by 95% due to the natural properties of that material sliding along 'itself' and those two adjustments would minimize your losses from those two factors.

Old concept with new materials and you've got a winner mate!

author: @cheerdiver
text:
While electric motors can exceed 95% eff, electric generators are on the order of 50% eff.  PLEASE, correct me if I'm wrong, w/ ref.
That's the electric car's dirty little secret, you never hear.  So why the desperation to use inferior technology, convenience?
While pneumatics have high reaction times, there's still thermodynamic loses due to the adiabatic cycle.
Hydraulics tend to exceed 90-98% eff, 'out of the box'.  To store energy, raise the elevation.  The driven components of a vertical turbine are usually at it's base.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sounds right to me mate - but let me say - it really doesn't matter what you say someone somewhere will argue with you so I tend to pick the figures as conservative or I just get a million posts

replies.author: @cheerdiver
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Point conceded.
While the sentiment may not be complete on my end, too many people get caught up in the magic of magnets (derived from magi).  With copper touching all time highs, two hydraulic pump turbines could be fabricated for the price of an electric, yielding four time the energy.
When power companies post efficiency numbers for hydraulic storage of energy (Irish), they make a sophist claim of 85% ENERGY efficient.  When they take 100Kw of electricity and pump water to a higher elevation, yes they do recover 85Kw of HYDRAULIC ENERGY.  They conveniently leave out converting that energy back to electricity.
Untrained people tend to have unrealistic expectations, when it comes to things they don't comprehend.  We should keep those expectations in check with reality, so as not to create fodder for P.T. Barnum.  Else it becomes a poor reflection of our knowledge, by the sophistry of con-artist.

author: @danielmierop662
text: As far as height issue, I agree with you that it can be a challenge. I see more and more ground based wide throat collectors as safer low / ground based with internal turbine. Not all experimenters have two buildings like a home and garage that just happen to form a 45 degree funnel of accelerated air ( prevailing winds) to place a turbine like your current project. Well I do and it got me watching all your videos. Also wanted to suggest an inflated tire tube surrounding the turbine input to increase or add a  venture / Bernoulli accelerating external air and then that would have an affect to pull more air past the turbine and strengthen rotation. Also rumor that air exit opening to be not more than 15 degrees. Somewhere there are YouTube videos on both subjects.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
One small hurdle with a vawt and a wheel and rope transmission system is that the pully system  needs rotate with the the changing direction of the wind if your turbine directly drives the top pully.   

One might not want to directly drive the top pully with the turbine. using some type of gear instead. I dont know what the name of it is, but there is  a certain type of gear system that will allow the top pully remain stationary with respect to the pole.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: distancing the genie from the turbine is certainly an explored idea mate - I am only looking at the power transmission here - I know there are systems for doing exactly what you say - but like you I can't remember the name lol

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  Putting the genie near the ground was someone else's idea. 😁

 I was just contemplating how to transmit mechanical power with a rope and pully where:  
A) the top pully is fixed to a wind turbine that rotates into the wind; or
B) the top pully is fixed to the pole, and the turbine that rotates into the wind uses this so far unnamed gear, to to transmit power to the top pully.

I would love to see RMS actually make a turbine and put it to use, hopefully one day he will come up with something worthy of putting on the roof. I have built a few over the last 30 years, but none were robust enough to last more than a year or two, so I have always fallen back on commercial models, be great to see something from RMS that rivals a Hugh Piggot design but I think hes a long way off, hopefully he proves me wrong.

author: @jasonhaymanonthedrawingboard
text:
Intriguing! I’m wondering if you can Improve on that. Magnetic chain perhaps?  Or a magnetised steel cable? Reduced friction is bound to boost efficiency. combine that with magnetic gears. You then could put a planetary gear set on a very long line. Hey presto liner transfer motor. 

Then you could ad as many transfer motors as you like to level up on the power. It allows for gearing up or down to prevent overwhelming a generator. Easy a terrawatt if spread over 5 miles. With multiple interfaces along its route. To think your using just a drill motor. If employed in somewhere were there are lots of cables. I guess I might be possible to use the thermodynamic principles. of its not how much energy it has but how far it goes?  Basically the further it goes the more people it can reach, the more interaction and the more times that energy gets used. Tai chi 101. Use the energy to best effect. 

I’m not going to cry overnuity but I’m sure if you add it all up it might be. Or pretty damn close.It’s something that they do with money that massively inflates the economy. Think of it like giant game of pass the parcel. The more hands it passes through the further It goes and the more people it will help. Remember nothing is ever created, nothing is ever destroyed. it just moves from point a to point b! Meaning everything is transmutable and transmissible. From one thing to another from one place to another. Efficiency is how easy it is to do that? A Nobel prize winning thought. but if you guy could work it out then you would be on to a winner.  Something borrowed, something blue a story of success is here for you! Go for it lads your on the right path for sure! Good luck!

But the last couple of videos discussion was putting it on the side of an existing building. Thereby omitting the need to build the Tower or building a structure on the roof.

In the last video some guys were talking about putting it on the roof flat, which would take advantage of the ramp of a angled roof.

Another guy talked about putting a set of blades on both sides of the center disc.

With this transmission system of a bicycle wheel, a two blade turbine that is flat on an angled roof could still power motor that would be separate on the roof and powered by this rope.

replies.author: @johndough8115
replies.text:
@reypolice5231 It would likely have to be vertical windmill methods, with extremely strong materials.. because wind turbulence on a building can be VERY extreme.  One day while driving in the city, I was stopped at a light.  There were at least 5 cars ahead of me.  The wind gushed hard, and every car in the line was forced downwards..  making a car-wave.  The amount of forces that pushed my car down that much, was astounding, and scary.   Being that wind gusts can be even stronger the higher up they are... the more such a device will need to be even more Robust.

 Maintenance would also be an issue, as you would need to use something like a window washers hoist, to be able to do any service on them.   Not sure how much mass such a hoist is designed to handle.

replies.text:
I could well be wrong here as at the time I was only 4 years old and so never allowed up to the top of our village windmill but I seem to think that there was a large circular gear up at the top of the windmill that a separate gear from the sails of the windmill drove.
A second fixed gear  underneath the big circular driven gear drove the machinery down stairs.
The dome of the windmill with the sails was on wheels so that it could all turn without need to  change the driving gear underneath the large wheel.
Hope that makes some sense

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: @calvingreene90 That makes much more sense than rope. With a VAWT yes a connecting axel is the best way to go if you don't want the generator on the mill. Bit confused, only a few episodes back Rob said he really liked putting magnets on the turbine, now its the opposite ? His current design is a HAWT, so intrigued to see what he's going to do with the rope?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: distancing the genie from the turbine isn't a new idea or my idea mate - it is being explored by others for the benefits I talked about - I would just look then up to answer that question - here we are only looking at a possible 'cheap' way of doing the actual power transmission

replies.author: @goldmagnet9013
replies.text: ​@ThinkingandTinkering I understand, and have used a plains style water pump in the past, very reliable. I do not understand how your rope transmission system would work with a HAWT and still be efficient, seems a foolish endeavour for many many reasons, but willing to learn. I think a lot of people would like to see one of your designs actually installed and running for a month or two.

author: @Geodesix
text: What about compressed air that runs a flywheel at the genset...?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Curtana really has the answer to that mate

author: @stevetobias4890
text: Looking back before looking forward, couldn't agree more. So much is forgotten in time and there are so many ideas simply lost in the ages. We need to unbox these lost ideas an modernise them into more efficient, working ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: 100% for sure mate

author: @Gainn
text: A third wheel on the outside of the loop as a tensioner and you've got a very stable system.

replies.author: @nitrousman8882
replies.text: or a third wheel inside the loop as a tensioner....  it's all relative

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text: @nitrousman8882 on a toothed system it's not so much of an issue, but on a friction system you want as much contact from the drive rope to the drive wheel, and inside pushing outwards can reduce contact area and increase slippage and instability unless you use quite a bit of tension.. outside pushing in increases contact area on the drive and pickup wheels (closer to the higher torque side is usually better) so reduces slippage and the likelihood of instability with less tension.

replies.author: @nitrousman8882
replies.text: @Gainn and with a reasonable sized pair of pulleys, you can “push” in and get better contact than you can, with a tensioner pulling outward…. Perhaps you can put some numbers with that.  I do agree that the toothed example is significantly different

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @bugz000
text: i seen photos of old "modernised" mills that still used water power to turn a huge wheel, but that power was then put through these long shafts running up and down the warehouse providing a small rotating axle at 90 deg every few feet for a pulley or shaft, these would be running all sorts of machinery, these were soon replaced by giant stationary engines, and soon replaced again by air-lines and compressors, but it was certainly used, high torque low speed, the losses are negligible :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is certainly an old idea mate - cheers

author: @davesharo1159
text: It would be interesting to see if multiple generation wheels could be used, snaking the rope round them like a serpentine belt. This may allow for better tensioning of the rope, as well as increased power generation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it certainly could mate - think cable car

author: @rogerwilson6367
text: Apparently the most efficient form of power transmission is the bicycle chain.

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text: 99%+ power transmission if correctly tensioned.

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835
replies.text:
Don't you love coming to the comments and seeing that what you meant to post was already said?
Chain and sprocket can hit 99% but real world it's more like 95-97%; Still, it's simple, inexpensive and all the engineering has been done for you.
A close second would be a toothed belt. Much more tolerant of outdoor use with nothing but a bit of a cover to keep random bits (or fingers) out. Now, if only the large sprockets weren't so very expensive. :D

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text: @matthewellisor5835 Would a V-belt setup work out cheaper? in this application the friction would probably be enough to get close to toothed belts.

replies.author: @rogerwilson6367
replies.text: @matthewellisor5835 I must have missed that if it was already said. My humble apologies. Tried to replay the video but it won't play for some reason.

replies.author: @matthewellisor5835

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, very interesting discussion. Early windmills directly drove a water pump below using gears and a drive shaft. Placing the heavy components on the ground make a lot more sense. We've been looking at this problem all wrong before because others forgot the old ways. And sometimes the old ways were the best ways of doing things. Brilliant!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: absolutely mate - like I say sometimes it is better to look back and that helps us look forward
